retrieved_docs.title: Symptomatic Carotid Artery Stenosis -- Introduction -- Timing of Carotid Endarterectomy
retrieved_docs.content: The timing of intervention for carotid stenosis is controversial because there is conflicting evidence about the risks and benefits of intervention performed at various time periods. The landmark trials North American Symptomatic Carotid Endarterectomy Trial (NASCET) and the European Carotid Surgery Trial (ECST), both published in 1991, showed that CEA for symptomatic carotid stenosis performed within 6 months of a symptomatic event reduces the rate of recurrent stroke, and most of the benefit was realized when CEA was performed within 2 weeks of the ischemic event. This led to recommendations that CEA be performed within 14 days of an ischemic event, with some guidelines using even stricter guidelines of CEA within 48 hours of the referring event. [5] Another study from the Swedish Vascular Registry (Swedvasc), published in 2012, suggested an increased risk of perioperative complications when CEA was performed within 48 hours of the ischemic event compared to CEA performed 3 to 7 days after the event (11.5% versus 3.6%). There was also no increased risk of embolic events in CEA performed at 8 to 14 days after the event. [5] [6] More recent guidelines favor first classifying the index event as either a TIA or stroke—then considering CEA within 48 hours if the initial event is a TIA. If the initial event is a stroke, intervention between 3 to 7 days can be considered. [4] An exception to waiting 48 hours after the initial event would be patients who have repetitive or worsening TIAs, also called crescendo TIAs. These patients likely benefit from early intervention to avoid further neurologic compromise. [7] Multiple patient-specific factors play into the decision of optimal timing for a CEA.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Endarterectomy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the indications for carotid endarterectomy. Describe the technique involved in performing a carotid endarterectomy. Review the complications associated with carotid endarterectomy. Explain how careful planning and discussion amongst interprofessional team members involved in the management of patients undergoing carotid endarterectomies will improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Carotid Sinus -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: There is an inter and intraindividual variation in the location of the carotid sinus. This knowledge of variations of the carotid sinus is quite essential for a surgeon before going for any interventions (like carotid endarterectomy, carotid stenting, etc.) at this site, as accidental stimulating it might lead to severe hypotension, bradycardia and rarely cardiac arrest. Four different sites of carotid sinus exist: Site 1 - starts in the distal common carotid artery and extends to the proximal internal carotid artery, which is the most common location (74.3%). Site 2 - starts in the distal common carotid artery and extends to both the proximal internal carotid artery and proximal external carotid artery (7.3%). Site 3 - starts in the distal common carotid artery with no extension after bifurcation (17.1%). Site 4 - starts in the distal common carotid artery and extends to the proximal external carotid artery, which is the least common location of all (1.22%). [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Carotid Bodies -- Physiologic Variants -- Common Carotid Artery Bifurcation Variance
retrieved_docs.content: The common carotid arteries usually bifurcate at the upper border of the thyroid cartilage. However, there are physiologic variants in specific individuals due to anatomical, genetic variations and also impact the success of certain surgical procedures to address disease states such as atherosclerosis. [11] In these cases, the variance of the location of the bifurcation of the common carotid artery could pose a surgical challenge in the setting of localized atherosclerosis where a distal bifurcation may be present.

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Endarterectomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: It is possible to screen asymptomatic patients with the use of carotid duplex ultrasonography (CDU), which can assess the degree of carotid stenosis. The severity of the obstruction correlates with carotid flow velocity: according to Poiseuille's Law, the narrower the lumen of the vessel, the higher the flow rate will be through the stenotic segment. Inaccuracies can be shown if there are tortuosities in the vessel, which may cause elevated velocities. Although CDU is useful in detecting hemodynamically significant stenosis, it has relatively low specificity for those patients with 50% to 60% stenosis. Other forms of screening include computed tomography angiography (CTA), and contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance angiography (CE-MRA). These imaging modalities help to assess other variables that can impact an individual's risk including plaque morphology, intracranial collateralization, and brain perfusion. CTA or CE-MRA information can be used alongside CDU to help determine an individual patient's risk profile when contemplating surgery. [5] [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Endarterectomy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Symptomatic women may be at increased risk of postoperative complications after CEA when compared to men, while outcomes and complications remain similar among the sexes for asymptomatic patients. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Carotid Arteries -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The recent improvement in the medical management of asymptomatic carotid artery stenosis has shown comparable rates to carotid endarterectomy for reduced stroke risk. As a result, medical management has become more favorable and is currently the advocated preference for patients with asymptomatic carotid artery stenosis. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: CEA was first reported by Eascott et al in 1954 and described in 1975 by DeBakey. [4] [18] Annually, 150,000 patients undergo CEA globally. [19] In the United States, the annual usage of the procedure decreased from 51.6 to 22.5 cases per 100,000 population from 2006 to 2020. [4] Carotid revascularization procedures have profound benefits if performed within 2 weeks of the index event. [4] Surgery is usually deferred in the initial 48 hours (except in crescendo TIAs or stroke in evolution), owing to increased risk of periprocedural thromboembolic events from plaque instability and vulnerability. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Carotid Artery Surgery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms of extracranial carotid disease are most often caused by embolization. Arterial emboli account for approximately one-quarter of strokes in Europe and North America, and 80% of these originate from atherosclerotic lesions in a surgically accessible artery in the neck. The most common lesion is at the bifurcation of the carotid artery. Lesions of atherosclerosis in the internal carotid artery occur along the wall of the carotid bulb opposite to the origin of the external carotid artery. Enlarging the bulb just distal to this major branch point creates a low wall shear stress area, resulting in flow separation and loss of unidirectional flow. This allows for greater interaction between atherogenic particles and the vessel walls at this site, accounting for the localized plaque at the carotid bifurcation. Transcranial Doppler (TCD) studies have shown that emboli are observed in approximately 20% of patients with moderate (>50% stenosis) lesions at the carotid bifurcation, and even higher rates are seen with more than 70% stenosis. The incidence and frequency of emboli are increased in patients who have recently become symptomatic. [1] [2] [3]

The intracranial arterial structure can reveal intracerebral atherosclerotic changes, providing an indirect site for such procedures. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected pathophysiology of precerebral arterial disease, basilar artery involvement, cerebral infarction, transient ischemic events, clinical presentation, and both medical and surgical management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1026
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component, indicating that the basilar artery is a child element within the overall architecture of intracranial arterial structures. This anatomical framework serves as a common site for the finding described as تصلب عصيدي في المخ, reflecting the presence of intracranial atherosclerotic disease within these vessels. Surgical management of vascular pathology in the head and neck region often involves an endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, a procedure that is indirectly related to the Structure of intracranial artery because interventions on extracranial vessels can affect or be associated with intracranial arterial anatomy. The endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck itself is classified under the broader procedural category استئصال باطنة الشريان (arterial endarterectomy), establishing a hierarchical relationship in which the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is a subset of general arterial endarterectomy. Understanding these anatomical, pathological, and procedural connections is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate surgical or endovascular treatments involving intracranial and extracranial arteries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 199
retrieved_docs.content: Therapeutic procedures for arteries (excluding the aorta) form a hierarchical group that includes carotid endarterectomy, generic arterial endarterectomy, aneurysm resection, pulmonary artery banding, and percutaneous coronary intervention performed through the skin. Carotid endarterectomy is a specific instance of arterial endarterectomy, and the endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels is classified as a subprocedure under the broader arterial endarterectomy category. The anatomical structure of the intracranial artery serves as a site where intracranial arterial atherosclerosis (تصلب عصيدي في المخ) can be identified, and it is also considered an indirect procedural site for endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels. This integrated view of vascular interventions and their related anatomical sites supports comprehensive planning of patient care and a clearer understanding of how different arterial procedures are related within the vascular therapeutic landscape.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 995
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery is identified as the anatomical site where تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial atherosclerotic disease) can be found. In addition, this arterial structure is regarded as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, indicating that surgical planning for endarterectomy may involve the intracranial arterial region even when the primary target is a head‑and‑neck vessel. The entity endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck is itself a specific instance of the broader surgical technique استئصال باطنة الشريان (endarterectomy of the arterial intima), reflecting a hierarchical relationship in which the head‑and‑neck endarterectomy procedure is a subcategory of general arterial intimal removal. Understanding these connections helps clinicians locate intracranial atherosclerotic lesions, consider appropriate surgical approaches, and recognize how specific endarterectomy procedures fit within the overall framework of arterial wall excision.

The SMQ grouping حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (arterial thrombosis and embolism cases) is a member of both the precerebral arterial occlusion/stenosis spectrum and the endarterectomy procedural domain, underscoring the therapeutic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: الشريانية (SMQ)), linking cardiac infarction to the same thrombo‑embolic mechanisms that produce cerebral infarcts. Collectively, occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries, basilar artery stenosis, thrombosis, and embolism, as well as non‑Q wave and subendocardial myocardial infarctions, represent a unified vascular pathology characterized by atherosclerotic change, thrombo‑embolic obstruction, and the common morphological outcome of infarction (الاحتشاء). These entities are interconnected through shared anatomical sites (intracranial arteries, anterolateral myocardium), common procedural interventions (arterial endarterectomy, endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels), and overlapping clinical classifications, providing a comprehensive framework for diagnosis, risk stratification, and management of thrombo‑embolic disease in both the cerebral and cardiac circulations.

Therapeutically, heparin is noted as an anticoagulant that may be employed to treat brain infarction resulting from embolic or thrombotic mechanisms. Together, these relationships integrate clinical presentations (syncope, asymptomatic occlusion), imaging findings (intracranial atherosclerosis), procedural interventions (endarterectomy), pharmacologic therapy (heparin), and hierarchical disease classification, providing a comprehensive view of how precerebral arterial occlusion or stenosis can manifest as various forms of cerebral infarction—sometimes silently—and how it fits within the broader neuro‑vascular disease framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery is identified as the anatomic location where تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis) can be observed. This arterial structure also functions as an indirect_procedure_site_of endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, meaning that endarterectomy procedures on head‑and‑neck vessels may be performed indirectly at this site. The specific surgical action termed endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck has a parent relationship to استئصال باطنة الشريان (arterial endarterectomy), indicating that it is a specialized form of the broader endarterectomy technique. Furthermore, استئصال باطنة الشريان is listed as having a member relationship with حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ) (cases of arterial thrombosis and embolism), placing the procedure within the clinical context of managing thrombotic and embolic arterial disorders. Together, these connections illustrate how intracranial arterial pathology, surgical intervention, and related thromboembolic conditions are interrelated in patient assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 771
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a child component, indicating that the basilar artery is a sub‑structure within the broader intracranial arterial system. This anatomical structure is identified as a finding site for تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial arterial sclerosis), highlighting its clinical relevance in the assessment of cerebrovascular disease. Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck is described as an indirect procedure site of the Structure of intracranial artery, meaning that surgical removal of the arterial intima in head‑and‑neck vessels may be performed in relation to this intracranial arterial region. The procedure Endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck has a parent relationship with استئصال باطنة الشريان (general endarterectomy), placing the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy as a specialized form of the broader endarterectomy technique. Finally, استئصال باطنة الشريان has member cases classified under حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ) (arterial thrombosis and embolism cases), indicating that endarterectomy is a therapeutic option for patients presenting with arterial thrombotic or embolic events. Together, these relationships delineate how the intracranial arterial anatomy, associated pathological findings, and endarterectomy procedures are interconnected in the diagnosis and management of cerebrovascular and arterial thrombo‑embolic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic procedures classified under “الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالشرايين (باستثناء الأورطي)” encompass several specific endarterectomy techniques. Among these, “استئصال باطنة الشريان السباتي” (endarterectomy of the internal carotid artery) and “استئصال باطنة الشريان الرئوي” (endarterectomy of the pulmonary artery) are distinct child procedures, as is the more general “استئصال باطنة الشريان” (endarterectomy of the arterial wall) and “استئصال العصيدة.” All of these interventions are organized within the same hierarchical category of arterial therapeutic procedures that exclude the aorta. The “Structure of intracranial artery” is identified as a site where pathological findings such as “تصلب عصيدي في المخ” (intracerebral atherosclerosis) may be observed. This arterial structure is also linked indirectly to “endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck,” indicating that head‑and‑neck endarterectomy procedures are relevant to the management of intracranial arterial disease, even though the procedure is performed on extracranial vessels. Furthermore, the category “الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالشرايين (باستثناء الأورطي)” is positioned as a subcategory of “تدخل للأوعية التاجية عن طريق الجلد” (percutaneous coronary intervention), reflecting the broader relationship between surgical arterial therapies and minimally invasive coronary interventions. Together, these relationships illustrate a comprehensive framework in which various endarterectomy techniques are organized, connected to intracranial arterial pathology, and related to percutaneous coronary treatment strategies.

Structural consequences of precerebral arterial narrowing include intracranial arterial sclerosis (تصلب عصيدي في المخ), a finding site of the Structure of intracranial artery. This sclerosis is directly linked to the underlying occlusive process and represents a target for surgical intervention. Endarterectomy of the vessel of head and neck (endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck) is identified as an indirect procedural site for addressing plaque within the intracranial artery. This procedure falls under the broader category استئصال باطنة الشريان (arterial endarterectomy), which is indicated for cases encompassed by حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ). retrieved_docs.content: an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy; it is classified as a vessel of the head and neck. Arteria basilaris itself is a documented site of occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries. The basilar artery is also the anatomic location where transient cerebral ischemia (نوبة إقفارية عابرة) occurs, representing a transient ischemic attack. Occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries encompasses basilar‑artery narrowing (تضيُّق الشريان القاعدي) and basilar‑artery thrombosis (خثار الشريان القاعدي), both linked to cerebral atherosclerosis and to the development of احتشاء دماغي. These vascular abnormalities are classified within central nervous system extracranial vascular diseases (أمراض وعائية إقْفارِيّة في الجهاز العصبي المركزي) and constitute a specific form of arterial occlusion (انسداد الشرايين). Embolism of the basilar artery (embolie; arteria basilaris) maps to the broader category of occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries and is a subtype of cerebral artery
